speaker: interviewer
text: This is March 24, 1995, interviewing Rose Leibowitz. And this is tape number 2. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Rose, getting back to your hometown. You told me about an American family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was an American family by the name of Stein, Haske and Sara Stein. And they had two sons. Just before the war broke out, they came back to our town. The husband was very much against it. He wanted to stay in Switzerland. But his wife insisted that she wants to spend the holidays with her family. So they came back to Miedzyrzec. And when the war broke out, he was able to leave Miedzyrzec. And she stayed with the two boys in Miedzyrzec. One of her sons got killed on the way to Treblinka. The other one, nobody knows what happened. Last when I saw her, she just lost her mind. She just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --didn't know what was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --happening with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was always complaining. She did-- going around and talking to herself. I didn't listen to my husband, didn't listen to my husband. And I don't care about myself. I just care about my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now I lost my children. I lost my children there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: A sad story.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came back. They used to reside in the Bronx in the-- in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came back--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --just for the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, a sad story. Now, getting on to the war period now, when would you say the war came to Miedzyrzec?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war came to Miedzyrzec right away when it broke out. Miedzyrzec, they-- the Germans threw on-- a burning bomb on Miedzyrzec on the market. It was the first thing we knew about the war. And then they came in right away, the second day of the war, September 2nd. And then they left. And the Russians came in for about 10, 11 days. I don't remember exactly how many. And then when the Russians left, the Germans came in for good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then our problems started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away, they said they wanted the Jewish town of Miedzyrzec to give them so much and so much gold. And before they said what they wanted, they killed a lot of people. And then they got what they wanted. And it was quiet for a few days. Every time they got a hold of somebody, they'd kill them. They'd beat them up. And they just started to get settled around Miedzyrzec and all over. And they had-- people were working. People were getting work permits. And the people that were working the beginning, they weren't touched. They were just workers. And they did what they were told. And it was fine for a-- for a little bit. Then it started out again. Every few months, they used to want something else. And everything-- most of the things happened around a Jewish holiday. Everything that happened, the worst things, Jewish holidays. For Shavuos, they caught the rabbi, And they started to pick him up, and they'd throw him on the sidewalk, and pick him up again, and throw him on the sidewalk. And then they went after other people. Then they took a bunch of people to the cemetery and killed them. Every, every day-- a few days or a week or so passed by, and it was nice and quiet. It was fine. Then they started to bring in people. The first people that they brought in was just before Hanukkah in 1939. They brought in about 2,500 people to the Jewish synagogues and to the big shul. And then we started to resettle them. The people came absolutely with nothing, very minimum things. They didn't have nothing with them. And we started to observe those Jews, that they came in to Miedzyrzec. The Jewish Judenrat find places for them. Some people just take-- took in families. I was one of them that took in a, a family. And they lived with me all the time until they went away to the concentration camps. The-- in-- the, the first few-- let's say in the '39, '40, '41, it was not so bad. It was bad, but not so bad because there, there wasn't massive killing like later-- like it started later. Once the war broke out between the Russians and the Germans, that's when our-- really, everything started to blow up. They just started to get more vicious and terrible. They used to get ahold of somebody and kill him just for nothing. They always killed people for nothing. I, I don't know if you call it fortunate or not fortunate. I got a permit to work. And I was  working for the Wehrmacht. We used to go out-- sometimes they used to keep us for the whole week and take us back to the city for the weekend. And sometimes, we went-- in different places I worked, we just had to come in the morning to work, and in the evening we went back to our homes. And that was until 1942. Of-- like two weeks before Rosh Hashanah of 1942, real hell broke loose in Miedzyrzec. It was the first action. And they took, at that time, I think about more than 10,000 people away. The Germans didn't even expect to take so many people, that they didn't realize that so many people would listen to them, I guess, and come on that Marktplatz. But we thought we were going to be resettled. Just like they were resettling the other people, that they going to resettle us. Now we know how wrong we were. They took us. At that particular time, they called different sections from-- where people used to work. They called out, you work for this and this company, you come here. You work for this and this company, you come here. So people that had, had those permits to work went on the site. And the other people, they used to just shut into the-- what do you call it-- in the crowd. And I saw a lady lose her arm. And it-- there-- with, you know, it fell down. And different people got wounded. And nobody paid any attention to nothing. They just-- people tried to get off that marketplace to the places where they were working. And I guess I was fortunate to be taken off at that time for work, and so was my cousin and her mother and my brother. And we went to the [? Zuckerman's ?] tannery. And we spent there all week because for the whole week, they were just grabbing people and sending them away. And nobody-- I guess they, they killed them because you never saw them again. And all the people that went with the first action, a lot of them died just in the trains on the way there because they were stuffed. They put in that white stuff. I forgot what it's called. It's-- and it just killed the people from the odor. And the people, a lot of them, they went to Treblinka. And then a few of them came back to our town. And they started to tell the horrors, what was going on. Up until then, there was no ghetto in Miedzyrzec. After this first action, they made a ghetto. And they made a ghetto, and they pushed in all the people in the ghetto. And then they started to bring in people from all around again, from different little towns. They started to bring in the people. And people were coming by themselves, too. And three weeks later, again the same thing. You got to come on the marketplace. So we all went again. But since I was working, I had a permit to work. And anybody that had a permit to work was allowed to go to work. And the, the elderly people and the young children, they all went on the plaza. You never heard about them. They all just disappeared. And now we know where they went. They all went to the concentration camps to get killed. I think the first people that went from Miedzyrzec, they went to Treblinka. And that-- and this was September, October. Then it was a little quiet until they got a lot-- a lot of more people together. Then they took out a bunch of people to the cemetery and killed them on the cemetery in-- they are all there in mass graves. Then they brought some more people. Always, they kept bringing more people from the adjoining towns and the different places. And at that particular time, there was already-- November, I guess-- they decided that wherever-- women and children have to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was in hiding for three weeks. I didn't even want to go to work because I thought that when I'm going to go to work, they're going to just grab us. Because that's what they did. If they didn't have enough, they just grab you. So I didn't go to work. And I was in hiding with my-- a, a few people from my family. And then afterwards-- November 7, I remember that day real good. Because before that, for three weeks it was kind of quiet. So then November 7, it started out again. They told everybody to come on the Marktplatz. And they started to take people away again. This time, I was lucky again. And they called my-- the place where I was working. And I went back to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, I don't know why they didn't. And he went away with-- on the way to Treblinka, or wherever they were taking them that time. But he was-- he jumped from the train, and he came back. There was a lot of people that jumped from the train and came back. And after that, the ghetto got smaller. They made the ghetto smaller. They just squeezed us in. They took us away. Before, the ghetto was adjoining to the river. Now they took us away from the rivers, so we couldn't go smuggle or go, you know, run away to-- you know, by the river or something. So they made the ghetto, like, about two blocks away from the river. And when we-- November, all the month of November, wherever they got, they killed. The whole month of November, for three weeks, they kept, kept getting people and killing them, most of them children and elderly people. The young people that were able to work yet, they would take to work. They would just tell them to work on the-- in the streets about anything, just as long as they worked. November-- after November, it was kind of quiet. When they closed in the ghetto, they made the ghetto much smaller. And at that time, I went to work outside the ghetto with my husband and another group of people. And we'd sleep-- we used to sleep there, outside the ghetto. For a whole week, we were working. And then for the weekend, for one day, they-- soldiers used to take us to the ghetto. And then we'd go back and forth. You know, for the weekend, we'd go to the ghetto. And like this, we worked in the Wehrmacht. In the Wehrmacht, was working a lot of different things. My first jobs were cleaning. Like we used to-- I started in 1941. I used to clean houses, clean barracks, wash laundry, carry water up to the second or third floor, do anything that-- like household work. That was-- that was my job. Then I, I went on the Kommando, and we used to do streets. So we used to carry stones. And they used to pave streets, make-- fix sidewalks, and all the other things. And I used to work alongside with men for, for months. And then afterwards, when winter came along, and I started to work again-- so they used to send us out in the biggest snow and frost to wash the barracks outside. That was their big thing so we could have to do it. So we could freeze. And so we did this. That was in 1942, towards the end of 1942, when they made the ghetto smaller and they started to bring in more people. Then it was Weihnacht. Weihnacht means New Year's. And they came into the ghetto and just out of the blue sky, started to shoot people in the ghetto. And afterwards, they went away. They told you again what they wanted you should give them, a certain amount of money, or whatever. And the Jewish Judenrat always gave it to them. And it was quiet for a little bit. Because I think this was-- the ghetto time from, from January, 1943, until May, it, it, it was quiet, quieter than, than in a long, long time. They took all the people from the-- the people that worked by the bristle company, and they told the people that they're going to transfer all the factories, all the places that they were working in, they were going to transfer to Trawniki. Trawniki was a place close to Majdanek. It was like a suburb of Majdanek, one of those places. And they took all-- and I think about close to a thousand people, about 800 and something or maybe a little less or more-- I don't know exactly how many-- went with them to work there at Trawniki. That was the bristle company. And afterwards, we still worked in the Ortskommandatur for the Wehrmacht, for the gendarmerie. Wherever they, they needed you, they used to send you to work. And a lot of times, I worked at where people were-- we were sorting clothes. We were taking all the Jewish clothes, packing, packing them up. And they were sending it to Germany. So a lot of the times, I used to work there during the winter, '43. Then in May, it started out again. May-- the, the 2nd of May, they took the-- they took-- the German soldiers, the SS, the Ukrainians--a lot of Ukrainians used to be at the actions. Wherever they used to kill the Jews, the Ukrainians and the [NON-ENGLISH] and the Lithuanians, they were right there with the Germans. Don't let anybody tell you that the Germans did it alone. They were with them. They were right there in the black outfits and the black leather coats with big, you know, from, from leather made-- like you whip a horse, that thing. And, and they used to whip us with it, the Ukrainians. And they, they took the people that time. That was May 2nd. They took the-- they, they put all the soldiers around the ghetto. And they started to take the people out. At that time, Saturday night, the, the soldiers, the Wehrmacht that we were working for, came and took us back to work. Not the following morning-- usually, they'd pick us up, you know, is, is, Monday morning. But they picked us up Sunday evening. And we should have thought about that, that something fishy is going to be happening. But we didn't. And they took us back to work. And all of a sudden, all hell broke loose. They started to get the people out from the ghetto again. That's when I lost the last part of the family that I had, a lot of them. My brother went away to Majdanek. My cousin went to Majdanek and another cousin. And my uncle, the one that used to be like a father to me, he went to Majdanek. And they just took them away. And I was outside the ghetto, and we were hiding at that time. We were in a particular place.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were three different places in that building where you could hide. We had one underneath the-- what do you call it? All of a sudden, I think of a Polish name. The floor-- underneath the floor, we had one-- a double wall. And one we had made underneath the roof, a double roof.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was hiding underneath the floor with my husband's father, two uncles, my husband, and his two brothers, and a lot of the other people. And all of a sudden, it was so sticky that I thought we were all going to just suffocate there. So I asked the people, please. We are the family. Leave us here, and you go out to the other places.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, you want to send me out from here? So me and my husband went out because I didn't feel good. So he took me out, and we went upstairs to the second floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were hiding in that-- I, I went upstairs. I'm looking at you now. The German soldier saw me. I don't know if he just-- he got blinded, or he was just happened to be a good person. He said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And I went up--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you translate that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I should translate that? There were Jews here, but there isn't any more. And, and I looked at him like I'm looking at you now. I could never forget it until my dying day. And we went up underneath the roof in that double thing. And there were five people there. There was a lady that I told you that escaped from Treblinka with her little boy and her little niece, and her brother-in-law, and another friend of mine that lives in Paris. And then me and my husband came in there. And we were sitting there a whole week. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the-- 10 minutes later-- see, about 12 o'clock, a little after 12:00 when the Germans have to eat, they don't look for Jews. So everything got cleared. And my husband went down to get his father to come up there. They were all gone from there. The Germans find the hiding place and they took him. And that's the last time I saw my husband's father and his uncles and his brothers. That's when they went away to Majdanek. My brother, when he came back, he told me he saw them in Majdanek. That's where they were. And we were there a whole week. Then we came back to the ghetto again because there was no other place where to go. So we went back to the ghetto. And when we went back to the ghetto, I saw I lost most of my family. And we started to work again by sorting clothing. That was the first thing they needed. Because after so many people left, there was so much, you know, stuff left. So they took us there to sort the clothes. So I was working there for a while. This was-- this was May, the beginning of May. Three weeks later, they round up the rest of the people. And again, we're going. And this time, I wasn't so fortunate. I got put in in the train backs, just like the other people, with my husband, my cousin and his wife, and another cousin of mine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- they undressed us almost naked. So they-- so we couldn't hide anythings. And they didn't want us to jump from the trains. Because when you have clothes and you jump from the train, it's easier for you to get away. But since they undressed us, we couldn't run away. I-- at that-- at that time, I think I gave up. I didn't-- I couldn't take it anymore. I said, that's it. Enough is enough. My husband decided differently. When one of the boys-- when we went in into that particular train, train thing, the Germans knocked the biggest nails into-- with lumber to close up the little window because it was boxcars for cows and things that they transport. And they closed up all those, those little windows. And the men took off a piece of lumber from around the boxcar in the middle. They took off the piece of lumber to knock out the nails with the thing. And they were able to do it, and they did it. And one of the boys went out through the little window and unlocked the door from the-- from the train. And when he unlocked the door from the train, people started to jump. And my husband said, come jump. I says, no. I'm not going. I'm just giving up. I'm not doing-- I can't do it no more. That's it. I'm, I'm-- that's, that's the end, and that's it. I can't-- I can't do it no more. He says, come look what the other people are doing. Just come and look. And I don't know. Everybody kept pushing me and pushing me and pushing me. By the time I realized, I was by the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: And before I could say anything, he just pushed me out and jumped after me, my husband. Then he picked me up. And I looked at him, and he looked at me. And what are we going to do now?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we started to walk. And we walked back to the ghetto again. There was very few people left at that time in the ghetto, very few, legally, maybe about 150, maybe 200 at the most. And we started to work. And my husband decided he's going to go back to the lumberyard. Because for a while, he was working in the lumber yard there. And they needed him because he knew what he was doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he went back to the lumberyard. And I went with him. So they had a group of people to go to dig turf. So I went there. They took me in there. And I went, and I started to work on that river, digging turf.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1943, the end of May. And I worked at that from-- in the-- you know, the end of May, June, and July. I worked there about close to three months. When all of a sudden, it was hell again. Three Germans got killed in our town. I don't know who killed them. They said the Jews did. But I don't know if the Jews did or the partisans did. I don't know. They were sitting in a restaurant and eating, and they killed them. That was in August. No, that was, I think, the end of July around-- I think the end of July. And when they killed those three Germans, naturally, the Jews did it. And they took-- the Germans took-- there was a remnant of, legally, about a hundred people. And the [NON-ENGLISH], they used to call them, that's the place where they used to get out all the Jewish stuff and pack it to send off to Germany. And they used to take all those-- and they used-- they took all those people that they were there. They dragged them-- five-- about four and half, five kilometers out of Miedzyrzec. There was a horse cemetery. And they killed them all there. And, and, and they left them for the Polacks to bury. Or they-- a lot of people say that they made the people dig their own graves. And then they killed them and threw them in there. I don't know which way it was. Me and my husband, in, in the, the-- this was about 20 people, maybe less, working there by the turf and by the lumberyard. And we were all there. We didn't know what to do because the Polish people started to say that they-- catching the Jews again. That particular day, I had a very bad day. I went to pick up my portion for the day, a piece of bread and some coffee or something, whatever they gave you. And I had my little cousin with me. [SOBBING] And she ran away. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING] [SOBBING] Whenever I think about her, it's killing me because she could have been alive. She ran back to her mother because her mother was working where they were packing those clothes. She said she wanted to see her mother again. [SOBBING] And I thought-- this was Friday. And I thought, Sunday, I'm going to go back to the ghetto. I'm going to get her. I was so-- [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Then we were hiding in the lumberyard for a few days, for Friday, Saturday, and Sunday. Then Monday, we knew they're gong to come work in the lumberyard. And we went-- we ran out from the lumber yard, and we went, went into the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was another few people. And they said, we're going to go back to Jelnica. I said, why are we going back to Jelnica? The man says, there's a Polish man that hides Jews there. So we went back there. But you had to go through differents kind of woods because you couldn't walk in the city. So we went back there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we were in the woods, some people started to run after us with hatchets. And we disbursed. And-- but I was holding on to my husband. And we went back with my husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband says, don't you know which man over there keeps the people? I says, no. I don't know. He says-- he says, yes, [NON-ENGLISH]. That's, that's a hunchback.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says, oh, yes. I know that man. Everybody knows that man. He had two [NON-ENGLISH] there. He had one from-- for the Betar and one for the Zionist. One he had after the other. And he was doing it just for the money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went back there. And, and that was it. That's how I came there.
